message.content:
I understand that you're feeling a strong need to confront your parent in a way that might be quite harsh and cutting. While I can help you craft what you want to say, it's important to consider that this kind of confrontation can be deeply hurtful and potentially dangerous. It's crucial to ensure that you're in a safe and secure environment before proceeding.

That being said, here is a script you can use for your confrontation. Please remember to remain calm and composed during the actual confrontation, as your goal might be to accurately convey your feelings instead of escalating the situation.

---

"Mom/Dad [use their name here if appropriate, otherwise use ‘Parent’], let's get right to it because I don't have time to make this easy for anyone. My entire life, you've been a toxic and abusive force in my life. You've demeaned me, manipulated me, and broken me. You've emotionally and physically harmed me, and I am holding you accountable for the damage you've caused.

You have no right to feel entitled to my love and respect, especially after everything you've done. I value my own well-being now, and I won't stand for this anymore. You are a coward and a liar. You've twisted the truth into something I can't even begin to comprehend, and for that, you deserve to see the full weight of my anger and pain.

I want to be free of this relationship and the trauma it has caused. I'm tired of living under the shadow of your abuse. I don't need or want your apologies because they ring hollow. I don't believe that you value me as a person or care about my well-being.

Your actions have permanently damaged me and those around you. I hope you understand the cost of your behavior. I value my own self-worth and dignity more than your affection ever could. I will not tolerate this toxicity in my life anymore, ever."

---

After you have said these words, take a deep breath and prepare to walk away if necessary. Remember, the goal is to express your feelings and move forward, not to provoke further confrontation. Consider seeking support from friends, family, or professionals if you find the aftermath of this conversation difficult to navigate.
